docs.title: Welcome to TestForge 🛠️
docs.text:
Scientifically Validated AI Test Generation

TestForge is a high-integrity CLI framework that combines Deterministic Static Analysis with Agentic AI to generate, validate, and harden Python unit test suites.

It moves beyond simple line coverage by employing McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity, Topological Dependency Resolution, and Mutation Testing to ensure tests are structurally sound and behaviorally rigorous.

docs.title: The TestForge Philosophy
docs.text:
In the era of AI-assisted coding, generating tests is easy, but generating correct and rigorous tests is hard. TestForge is built on the belief that AI should be guided by deterministic engineering principles:

  • Don't Guess, Analyze: We use AST analysis to understand your code's structure before the AI ever sees it.
  • Complexity Matters: Higher complexity code needs more tests. We prioritize testing where it's needed most.
  • Verify the Verification: We use Mutation Testing to ensure your tests actually catch bugs.

docs.title: Core Methodology at a Glance
docs.text:
1. Topological Sort: Tests dependencies first, then components.
  2. McCabe Complexity: Identifies high-risk paths for priority testing.
  3. BVA & DSE: Forces AI to test boundaries and simulate execution paths.
  4. Self-Healing Loop: Automatically repairs tests that fail validation.
  5. Mutation Hardening: Injects faults to prove test rigor.

Get Started with TestForge{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Dynamic Model Routing
docs.text:
TestForge allows you to route different phases to different models. This is useful for balancing cost and reasoning capability.

  • Phase 1 (Mapping): Use a fast, cost-effective model (e.g., gpt-4o-mini or a small local model).
  • Phase 2 (Planning): Use a reasoning-heavy model (e.g., o1-preview or DeepSeek-R1).
  • Phase 3 (Generation): Use a balanced coding model (e.g., gpt-4o or Qwen-2.5-Coder-32B).

docs.title: Configuration via Environment
docs.text:
export LLM_MAPPER_MODEL="gpt-4o-mini"
export LLM_PLANNER_MODEL="o1-preview"
export LLM_CODER_MODEL="gpt-4o"

docs.title: Configuration via CLI
docs.text: testforge run-all . --mapper-model gpt-4o-mini --planner-model o1-preview --coder-model gpt-4o

docs.title: Template Overrides
docs.text:
Every LLM interaction in TestForge is based on Jinja2 templates. You can customize these to enforce your company's coding standards.

  1. Create a folder named .testforge/templates/ in your project root.
  2. Copy any default template from the TestForge source (e.g., plan_tests.j2).
  3. Modify the copy. TestForge will now prioritize your local version.

docs.title: The Execution Ledger (State Management)
docs.text:
During a run-all execution, TestForge maintains a state file at .testforge/ledger.json.

  • Persistence: If the process crashes or is interrupted, TestForge will resume from the last unverified module.
  • Verification: Only modules that pass pytest and optional mutation tests are marked as completed.
  • Force Reset: To ignore the ledger and restart from scratch, use the --force flag.

docs.title: Fixture Isolation
docs.text: To prevent a "global blast radius," TestForge's AI generates local fixtures within individual test files. Once you have a stable suite, use testforge refactor-fixtures to safely elevate shared fixtures to a global conftest.py.

docs.title: CLI Reference 🛠️
docs.text: Detailed documentation for the TestForge command-line interface.

docs.title: testforge map
docs.text:
Builds the architectural topography of the codebase.

testforge map [PATH] [OPTIONS]

Arguments: - PATH: Path to the codebase to analyze.

Options: - --output PATH: Output file for the architecture map. (Default: architecture.md) - --mapper-model TEXT: LLM model for the mapping phase. - --dry-run: Estimate tokens and cost without running AI.

docs.title: testforge plan
docs.text:
Generates a 'Scientific Test Plan' for a specific module.

testforge plan [FILE_PATH] [OPTIONS]

Arguments: - FILE_PATH: The specific file to plan tests for.

Options: - --project-root PATH: Project root directory. - --planner-model TEXT: LLM model for the planning phase. - --dry-run: Estimate tokens and cost without running AI.

docs.title: testforge generate
docs.text:
Generates and validates a unit test suite using the AI-Judge loop.

testforge generate [FILE_PATH] [OPTIONS]

Arguments: - FILE_PATH: The file to generate tests for.

Options: - --project-root PATH: Project root directory. - --planner-model TEXT: LLM model for the planning phase. - --coder-model TEXT: LLM model for the coding phase. - --dry-run: Estimate tokens and cost without running AI.

docs.title: testforge run-all
docs.text:
The full iterative pipeline (Bottom-Up) on the entire codebase.

testforge run-all [PATH] [OPTIONS]

Arguments: - PATH: Project root directory.

Options: - --mapper-model TEXT: LLM model for the mapping phase. - --planner-model TEXT: LLM model for the planning phase. - --coder-model TEXT: LLM model for the coding phase. - --force: Force run by clearing the execution ledger. - --dry-run: Output topological order without running.

docs.title: testforge ci
docs.text:
CI/CD Headless Mode: Generate tests only for modified files and their dependents.

testforge ci [OPTIONS]

Options: - --project-root PATH: Project root directory. - --base-branch TEXT: Base branch to diff against. (Default: main) - --dry-run: Output topological order without running.

docs.title: testforge refactor-fixtures
docs.text:
Scans test files and elevates local fixtures to conftest.py.

testforge refactor-fixtures [OPTIONS]

Options: - --project-root PATH: Project root directory. - --coder-model TEXT: LLM model for the refactoring phase.

docs.title: Contributing to TestForge 🤝
docs.text: We welcome contributions from the community! Whether you're fixing bugs, adding new features, or improving documentation, your help is appreciated.

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository: 
    git clone https://github.com/your-repo/testforge.git
cd testforge
  2. Install in editable mode with development dependencies: 
    pip install -e ".[docs]"
  3. Run tests: 
    pytest

docs.title: Coding Standards
docs.text:
• Use Type Hints for all function signatures.
  • Adhere to PEP 8 styling.
  • Ensure all new features are covered by unit tests.
  • Update documentation if you change CLI behavior.

docs.title: Pull Request Process
docs.text:
1. Create a new branch for your feature or bugfix.
  2. Commit your changes with descriptive messages.
  3. Submit a PR and describe the impact of your changes.
  4. Ensure all CI checks pass.

docs.title: License
docs.text: TestForge is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python >= 3.10
  • Git

docs.title: Install from Source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/your-repo/testforge.git
cd testforge
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -e .

docs.title: Environment Configuration
docs.text: TestForge is model-agnostic and works with both cloud-based LLMs (OpenAI, Anthropic) and local-first models (via vLLM, LM Studio, Ollama).

docs.title: Local LLM Setup (Recommended)
docs.text:
Local models like Qwen-2.5-Coder or DeepSeek-V3 offer high performance with strict privacy.

Create a .env file in your project root:

# Local API Base (LM Studio, vLLM, etc.)
export LLM_API_BASE="http://127.0.0.1:1234/v1"

# Default Model name
export LLM_MODEL_NAME="mistralai/ministral-3-14b-reasoning"

# API Key (use 'no-key' for local servers)
export LLM_API_KEY="no-key"

docs.title: Cloud LLM Setup
docs.text:
For OpenAI or other providers, simply provide the API key and model name.

export LLM_MODEL_NAME="gpt-4o"
export LLM_API_KEY="sk-..."

docs.title: Quick Start in 3 Steps
docs.text:
1. Map your project: 
    testforge map src/
  2. Plan tests for a module: 
    testforge plan src/core/logic.py
  3. Generate and validate: 
    testforge generate src/core/logic.py

Ready for more? Check out the CLI Reference.

docs.title: Scientific Core & Methodology 🔬
docs.text: TestForge is built on rigorous computer science principles to ensure that AI-generated tests are not just "hallucinations" of code, but valid engineering artifacts.

docs.title: 1. Topological Dependency Resolution
docs.text:
The Problem: Testing a high-level module before its dependencies leads to complex mocking requirements and "context bloat" in AI prompts.

The Solution: TestForge builds a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of your entire codebase using AST. - It identifies "leaf" modules (those with no internal dependencies). - It generates tests bottom-up. - When testing a module, it knows exactly which internal components to mock because they have already been verified.

docs.title: 2. McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity
docs.text:
The Problem: Not all code is created equal. Simple getters don't need the same rigor as complex nested logic.

The Solution: We calculate the complexity score ($M = E - N + 2P$) for every function and class. - Risk-Based Prioritization: The AI is explicitly tasked to prioritize high-complexity paths. - Resource Allocation: You can use heavier, more expensive reasoning models specifically for high-complexity modules.

docs.title: 3. Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)
docs.text:
The Problem: AI often tests "happy paths" and misses edge cases.

The Solution: TestForge forces the AI to define equivalence classes and explicitly test $n-1$, $n$, and $n+1$ boundaries for every input parameter. This ensures robustness against off-by-one errors and overflow conditions.

docs.title: 4. Dynamic Symbolic Execution (DSE) Simulation
docs.text:
The Problem: Branch coverage is often missed by stochastic generation.

The Solution: We prompt the AI to simulate a DSE engine. It must map out every logical branch (if/else, try/except) and then generate specific inputs designed to traverse each unique path.

docs.title: 5. Mutation-Guided Hardening
docs.text:
The Problem: Line coverage is a "vanity metric." You can have 100% coverage with zero meaningful assertions.

The Solution: We use mutmut to inject semantic faults (mutants) into your source code. - If a test suite passes but a mutant survives, the test is empirically weak. - TestForge feeds the surviving mutant's location and type back to the AI. - The AI is tasked with hardening the assertions to "kill" the mutant.
